title: First Night
html:
The light went in stages. First the patches through the canopy thinned and the forest floor went from dappled to flat grey, then the grey deepened until I was reading distance by sound instead of sight. The birds had stopped about 20 minutes before full dark, which was worth noting. Either birds here had normal light-sensitivity or something that moved at dusk had trained them to go quiet early.

I kept my back to the rock and did not move.

Maya had taken the second watch without discussion, somewhere around the 6-hour mark. She had tapped my shoulder once, held up two fingers, and pointed at herself. I had moved to the overhang and sat with my back to the rock face and closed my eyes. I did not sleep deeply. I do not sleep deeply in unfamiliar environments, which used to bother me and now does not.

Soo-Yun slept properly. She had curled on her side with her rain jacket pulled over her shoulders and her school bag under her head and gone out fast, the way teenagers can, like sleep is a switch and not a process. The folding knife was in her hand. Not held like she planned to use it. Held the way you hold something you do not want to lose in the dark.

I noted that and did not comment on it.

Maya woke me for my second watch around what I estimated was the four-hour mark. The forest was fully dark by then. Whatever passed for a moon here was either new or behind clouds I could not see. I could make out the pale line of the rock face to my left and the slightly less dark shape of open ground in front and nothing beyond that.

The system timer when I checked it read 39:44:02.

I sat with the machete and I listened.

The forest at night has a different vocabulary than the forest in daylight. Daytime noise is ambient and constant, something you filter out. Night noise is specific. A sound at night means something made it. I had been sitting for maybe 30 minutes, cataloguing, when I heard the first one.

Not a branch this time. Lower. A press of weight on soft ground, the kind of compression that comes and holds before it lifts. About 12 meters out, slightly right of center from the clearing edge.

I did not move.

It moved again. Same sound, slightly closer. 10 meters, maybe. Patient.

Whatever this was, it had been here before. It knew the position. It was not stumbling on us. It was coming back.

I set my left hand on Soo-Yun's shoulder without looking at her, slow pressure, and felt her come awake under my palm. She did not make a sound. I held two fingers against her arm: wait. She went still.

Maya was already awake. I could tell by her breathing. She had the branch across her knees.

The sound stopped about 8 meters out.

Then a voice said, quietly and in a language I did not speak, something with the shape of a question.

I stayed still.

The voice again. Same language. Same shape. Then, slower, in heavily accented standard: 'You. Are you hurt.'

Not a question. The intonation was flat. Either the speaker did not know how to make questions in this language or they were being careful about the volume.

I turned toward the sound without standing.

"No," I said. Same volume.

A pause. Then a single footstep, deliberate, announcing itself. Then another. A figure resolved out of the dark at the edge of the clearing, moving slowly with both hands visible and away from their body.

Short. Compact. Moving like someone who had been moving through this forest for longer than a few hours. They stopped about 5 meters out and crouched down, which put their head below the line where a threat would carry it.

"I am alone," they said. "I smelled your fire."

I looked at Maya. She looked at me. Neither of us had lit a fire.

'Cache,' I thought. 'The firestarter block. It has a smell when it degrades in open air.'

"There is no fire," I said.

A pause. Then something like a short exhale. 'The block. Yes. I know the smell.'

They knew the caches. Which meant they had been in the tutorial long enough to find one, or they had information we did not.

I looked at the shape crouched in the dark. Both hands still visible. Weight on the balls of their feet, not the heels. Ready to move but not loaded to spring.

"How long have you been out here?" I asked.

"Since the start," they said. "Same as you."

That was not the same as us. We had been in the sealed room. This person had the movement of someone who had been navigating the forest since the tutorial opened, not someone who had spent two hours in a box first.

I filed the discrepancy and did not name it out loud.

"What do you want?" I asked.

Another pause. Longer.

"Water," they said finally. "I ran out of tablets three hours ago."

Her name was Pita. She was from an island country I had no descriptor for yet, short and brown-skinned with close-cropped hair and the particular kind of quiet that comes from being alone for a sustained period and recalibrating back to other people. She sat at the edge of the overhang with her back angled so she could see both the open ground and us, which I noted because I had chosen my own position for the same reason.

I gave her one tablet and watched her walk back to the stream in the dark without hesitation. She knew exactly where it was. She had been watching us since before we settled here.

Maya watched her go.

"She knew the stream was there," Maya said, quiet.

"Yes."

"And she waited until now to approach."

"Yes."

Maya turned that over. She did not say what she concluded. I liked that about her.

Pita came back with water in a container I had not seen her carrying, a flat-sided flask that had appeared from inside her jacket. She drank half of it standing, then sat back down at the overhang edge and looked at the three of us in sequence.

"How many in your group?" she asked.

"You can see how many," I said.

A thin pause. 'Fair.'

Soo-Yun was watching Pita the way she watched everything — with full attention and no expression of what she was making of it. Pita noticed. She looked at Soo-Yun directly.

"You found the cache," Pita said. Not a question.

Soo-Yun glanced at me. I did not signal anything. She looked back at Pita.

"Yes," Soo-Yun said.

"The moss tell. Most people walk past it."

Soo-Yun did not respond to that. She filed it the same way I would have, without showing the filing.

I looked at Pita. 'What class are you.'

She met my eyes. A beat.

"Scout," she said. "You?"

"Nothing yet."

She looked at the machete. 'You subdued the kappa at the clearing.'

"You saw that."

"I was in the trees."

So she had been there from the beginning. Before we entered the forest. She had come through the transparent wall separately and gone straight into the canopy while everyone else was standing in the clearing reacting to the kappa. I thought about what that meant: she had looked at the kappa and the crowd and made a calculation in the same moment I had, and her calculation had been different. Mine had been to stay and engage. Hers had been to disappear.

Neither calculation was wrong.

"Why approach now?" I asked.

She looked at the open ground for a moment. 'You did not light a fire.'

I waited.

"Most groups light a fire the first night. Signal. Everything in this forest that hunts at night moves toward fire. You did not light one." She looked at me. "That was a decision."

It had been. I had not explained it because Maya and Soo-Yun had not asked, and explaining decisions you have not yet executed is a waste of everyone's time.

"The large group," I said. "Northwest."

"They lit two," Pita said. "I heard something move toward them about an hour ago. I did not stay to watch."

I turned that over. Two fires and whatever lived in this forest hunting by light. The loud man's orbit was 200 meters away and had just become a much more immediate variable.

Maya's pen was moving in her notebook. She had not asked permission to write this down and I had not told her to stop.

The fire problem came up anyway around the 10-hour mark. Not from choice.

Soo-Yun's temperature dropped. I noticed it in her breathing first, then in the set of her shoulders. She had the rain jacket but the forest floor was cold against the rock and the overhang channeled air rather than blocking it. She was not shivering yet. She would be.

I looked at the firestarter block and then at the open ground.

Pita was watching me do the math.

"Small fire," she said quietly. "Inside the overhang. The rock will diffuse the light. Anything approaching from the front will not see it until it is already in the clearing."

Which gave us exactly the same warning distance as no fire. The trade was warmth for visibility risk, and the overhang geometry shifted the equation enough to be worth it.

"Can you do it quietly?" I asked.

"Yes."

She did. She had dry material I did not know she was carrying, birch bark curled into a cylinder, and she had the firestarter lit and the bark burning and a small teepee of wood feeding it in under two minutes. The light was minimal, lower than a candle at the opening of the overhang, and the rock behind us bounced it deeper in rather than out.

Soo-Yun moved closer to it without being told. The shivering potential eased out of her shoulders.

I watched the open ground.

Nothing moved.

Maya set her notebook down and looked at the fire.

"My husband would have lit one immediately," she said. "He would have said it was for morale."

It was the first time she had mentioned him since the restaurant. The first time she had mentioned him at all.

Nobody responded. She had not said it for a response.

"He is not here," she said after a moment.

Still flat. The same tone she had used to describe her Observer class. Like she was reading a fact off a list and checking whether it still hurt.

It still did. I could see it in the small stillness around her eyes. She had known it would, and she had said it anyway, which meant she was testing something. Testing whether saying it out loud made it easier to carry. That is a thing people do.

I did not say anything. Pita did not say anything. Soo-Yun glanced at Maya and then looked at the fire.

After a minute Maya picked up her notebook again.

The fire burned low and steady. Pita fed it in pieces, one stick at a time, never letting it climb.

I took watch again around what I estimated was midnight. The forest was as quiet as it was going to get. Whatever had moved toward the loud man's fires had either finished its business or moved on. I had not heard screaming, which could mean anything.

Pita settled with her back to the far side of the overhang and slept in the efficient way of someone who has learned to sleep in increments. Maya slept sitting up. Soo-Yun was already out, deeper this time, the knife no longer in her hand but beside it.

I looked at the four of us and did the accounting.

One machete. One branch. One folding knife. One Scout class with dry tinder and forest knowledge and a flask. One Observer with a notebook full of data I had not fully read. One 16-year-old who noticed things adults walked past. And me, with Perception 12 and no class and a professional smile that worked on monsters approximately as well as it worked on difficult customers, which was: not at all, but it bought time.

Forty hours left.

The unknown watcher from the afternoon had not returned. I did not take that as good news. Things that test a position once and then go quiet are either satisfied or planning.

I watched the open ground and kept that thought to myself.

---

【"Tutorial Chapter 1: Hour 10 Update"】

【"Participants Remaining: 84 / 103"】

【"Reminder: Survival is the objective. Everything else is optional."】

---

19 people gone in 10 hours. I did not know the breakdown. Injury, predation, the loud man's fires. Maybe some had found a way out I had not found. Maybe the system counted differently than I was counting.

84 left. I looked at the number and the number looked back.

'Not a problem I can solve tonight.'

I set it aside and watched the dark.
